BlackLabSnippetParser contains extractCollocateFromXmlByPosition, then introduces extractLemmaAt as 'the canonical method for positional lemma extraction' that directly delegates to extractCollocateFromXmlByPosition — the old method name is kept alongside the new canonical one with no @deprecated marker.

No @Deprecated annotations appear anywhere in the codebase. No 'DEPRECATED', 'remove after', or 'legacy' TODO/FIXME comments found.
No mixed-language (JS/TS) or old-vs-new framework patterns; the project is a single Java codebase with a consistent library stack (BlackLab, SLF4J, fastjson2, JUnit 5).

findCollocations in BlackLabQueryExecutor has a three-branch pattern format detection (contains '%s', starts with '[', else fallback with logger.warn) that suggests gradual migration from old pattern formats; the fallback branch emits a warning identifying it as a grammar config bug.

Production package inventory: root (1 file: Main.java), api (5 files), config (2 files), indexer.blacklab (1 file), query (5 files), tagging (1 file), utils (4 files), viz (1 file) — 20 production files total. Project is small; flat structure is generally appropriate at this scale.

SemanticFieldExplorer (query package) hosts 7 inner result classes (ExplorationResult, DiscoveredNoun, CoreCollocate, ComparisonResult, AdjectiveProfile, ExploreOptions, Edge). All are imported by ExplorationHandlers in the api package — cross-package inner-class coupling from api→query.
SemanticFieldExplorer class Javadoc explicitly notes: 'These should be extracted to a dedicated model/ package in a future refactor (see issue 28bc8918 / a430f585)' — coupling is self-acknowledged.

PatternSubstitution is placed in api package. It imports pl.marcinmilkowski.word_sketch.utils.CqlUtils and provides pure CQL string manipulation (extractXposFromConstraint, escapeForRegex, substituteCollocate). It has no HTTP imports and no dependency on HttpExchange. Affinity is with utils, not api.

BlackLabSnippetParser is in query package and provides both XML parsing utilities (extractLemmaFromMatch, extractPosFromMatch) and structural pattern analysis (findLabelPosition) — mixed concerns but all directly supporting the BlackLab query pipeline, so the placement is defensible.

tagging package contains only SimpleTagger.java — a single-file package. The tagger is not referenced by any production code in api or query (based on absence in seed files and source listing). May be a thin wrapper placeholder.

viz package contains only RadialPlot.java — a single-file package. RadialPlot is referenced by SketchHandlers.handleVisualRadial so it is actively used, but the placement in a separate viz package for a single file has low navigational value in a 20-file project.
indexer.blacklab package contains only BlackLabConllUIndexer.java — a single-file nested package. Given that indexing is a primary command in Main.java, this placement is clear and appropriate.

utils package contains CqlUtils, LogDiceCalculator, LongIntHashMap, PosGroup — four cohesive low-level utilities. PosGroup (an enum) could be in config since GrammarConfigLoader uses it, but the utils placement is not harmful.
No root-level stragglers beyond Main.java, which is the correct entry point placement.

BlackLabQueryExecutor constructor performs heavy I/O at construction time: 'this.blackLabIndex = BlackLab.open(new File(indexPath))' — this opens a BlackLab index which involves file system access, memory mapping, and potentially significant latency. This is declared throws IOException, so failures surface eagerly, but it couples object creation to I/O availability.

SemanticFieldExplorer(String indexPath) convenience constructor creates a new BlackLabQueryExecutor(indexPath) internally. In Main.handleServerCommand, the server already creates 'QueryExecutor executor = new BlackLabQueryExecutor(indexPath)' and passes it to WordSketchApiServer. WordSketchApiServer then creates SemanticFieldExplorer(executor) using DI — correct path. However, the String-path constructor exists and is callable, risking double-open if used outside this path.

SemanticFieldExplorer constructor comment: 'Prefer SemanticFieldExplorer(QueryExecutor) (dependency injection) for testability and to avoid double-open of the same index (see issue 297b2b52).' — the risk is acknowledged but the constructor is not guarded.

GrammarConfigLoader constructor reads the grammar JSON file at construction time (Files.readString or Files.exists + Files.readString). This is appropriate I/O for a config loader; it is declared throws IOException.

GrammarConfigLoader.createDefaultEnglish() calls System.getProperty('grammar.config', 'grammars/relations.json') — this reads a system property at call time (a static factory method), not at class-load time. No class-level static initializers read environment state.

No module-level (class-level static initializer) side effects observed: no static { } blocks performing I/O, no static fields initialized via database/network/file calls, no circular init dependencies.
BlackLabSnippetParser static final Pattern fields (LEMMA_ATTR, XPOS_ATTR, etc.) are compiled at class-load time — pure computation, no I/O, no external state dependencies.
LongIntHashMap, LogDiceCalculator, CqlUtils, and PosGroup have no static state beyond constants.

Main.handleServerCommand creates BlackLabQueryExecutor and GrammarConfigLoader sequentially at startup, both outside any static context — initialization is ordered and explicit, not hidden in class loading.

QueryResults.ConcordanceResult has an 11-parameter primary constructor with 7 fields documented as nullable (rawXml, lemma, tag, word, docId, collocateLemma, and by extension frequency/logDice are 0 when collocateLemma is null) — the type does not express which fields are valid in which context

BlackLabQueryExecutor.findCollocations silently falls back to a wildcard pattern when cqlPattern does not match any expected format: 'logger.warn ... Falling back to wildcard query' and continues execution instead of throwing — callers are not informed of the degraded behavior

QueryExecutor interface has good precondition/postcondition Javadoc for all methods; getExecutorType() has a default implementation that is honest and useful

BlackLabSnippetParser has two methods that iterate LEMMA_ATTR and return the last match: extractLemmaFromMatch (returns lowercased last lemma) and extractCollocateLemma (returns last lemma without lowercasing) — functionally nearly identical with no clear semantic distinction expressed by the different names

BlackLabQueryExecutor.findCollocations contains a redundant second null check: after the block that assigns collocateLemma = lastSpace >= 0 ? trimmed.substring(lastSpace + 1) : trimmed, a second 'if (collocateLemma == null || collocateLemma.isEmpty())' check is performed — the preceding assignment can never produce null (trimmed is from identity.trim()), only empty string

SemanticFieldExplorer.compare() computes minScore = Arrays.stream(scores).min().orElse(0.0) and stores it in a local variable, but minScore is never read in any subsequent computation — variance uses avgScore, commonality uses avgScore, distinctiveness uses maxScore only

RelationConfig.substituteHeadword in GrammarConfigLoader duplicates CQL bracket-walking (its own while-loop for '[', ']', and numbered prefix detection) that is already implemented in CqlUtils.splitCqlTokens, which exists specifically as a shared primitive for this purpose

BlackLabQueryExecutor.collectHits initializes String leftText = '', matchText = '', rightText = '', xmlSnippet = ''; String collocateLemma = null; and then immediately overwrites all five inside 'if (conc != null)' — the empty defaults are dead initialization that is never observed since records are created from the post-if-block values in both branches
LongIntHashMap logic is clean and correct: linear probing, power-of-two capacity, proper EMPTY_KEY sentinel, rehash on threshold — no spurious conditions
LogDiceCalculator.compute handles edge cases cleanly with explicit guards: headwordFreq <= 0, collocateTotal <= 0, dice <= 0 before the log call

All handler methods (SketchHandlers, ExplorationHandlers) build API responses as Map<String, Object> rather than typed DTOs — no compile-time validation of response shape; any misspelled key or wrong value type is invisible until runtime

QueryResults.ConcordanceResult has 7 fields documented as nullable (rawXml, lemma, tag, word, docId, collocateLemma, and indirectly frequency/logDice) in a class that declares them all as non-Optional String/long/double — the Java type system says they are always present but they are not

BlackLabQueryExecutor.buildAndRankCollocates accepts Map<String, String> posMap that can be null (called with null in executeSurfacePattern and findCollocations) — a nullable parameter in a non-Optional signature

GrammarConfigLoader.parseRelationType returns null when the relation_type value is absent or unrecognized, then RelationConfig record holds a nullable RelationType field — callers of relationType() must null-check even though RelationType is a well-defined enum

ExploreOptions is a record with all primitive/non-null fields — clean type discipline for configuration bundles

simplePattern local variable in ExplorationHandlers.handleSemanticFieldExplore — 'simple' is relative and says nothing about the variable's role; it is a fallback/reverse-lookup pattern, so reverseCollocatePattern or fallbackPattern would communicate intent

BlackLabSnippetParser.extractLemmaFromMatch and extractCollocateLemma perform the same operation (find last lemma in XML); the names suggest different roles (head vs collocate) but both extract the last lemma — a naming mismatch between intent and implementation

BlackLabQueryExecutor wraps InvalidQuery and all low-level exceptions into IOException with original cause preserved throughout (lines 138, 177, 208, 242, 425, 502) — consistent chaining pattern in the query layer.
WordSketchApiServer routes catch IOException and translate to HTTP 500 via sendError() — consistent at the API boundary for most handlers.

getTotalFrequency in BlackLabQueryExecutor: 'catch (Exception e) { logger.warn(...); return 0; }' — broad catch swallows the exception and silently returns 0, making callers unable to distinguish index failure from absent lemma; logDice scores become silently incorrect.
Main.java shutdown hook: 'catch (IOException e) { // Ignore }' — silent swallow of close() failures at cleanup boundary.

WordSketchApiServer inconsistently catches exceptions across sibling route wrappers: /api/semantic-field/explore and /api/semantic-field/explore-multi wrap both IOException and Exception, while /api/semantic-field, /api/semantic-field/examples, and /api/concordance/examples wrap only IOException — an unchecked RuntimeException from those handlers would propagate to the raw HttpServer and crash the request silently.

handleBcqlQueryPost catches generic Exception and sends HTTP 400 ('BCQL query error: ' + e.getMessage()) regardless of whether the failure is a parse error or an I/O failure — conflates client error (400) with server error (500).
ExplorationHandlers consistently catches NumberFormatException and returns 400 with message — correct client error handling at validation boundary.
All Java class names use PascalCase, all method names use camelCase — standard Java naming conventions followed without drift across all packages.
Package organisation is coherent: api/, config/, query/, utils/, indexer/blacklab/, tagging/, viz/ — each has a single clear responsibility.
ExplorationHandlers and SketchHandlers are symmetric: package-private, constructor-injected, methods named handle*() — consistent handler convention.

Result DTOs split across two locations: ExplorationResult, DiscoveredNoun, CoreCollocate, ComparisonResult, AdjectiveProfile, Edge are inner public static classes of SemanticFieldExplorer (query package), while WordSketchResult and ConcordanceResult live in a separate QueryResults class (also query package). ExplorationHandlers (437 lines) and SketchHandlers (456 lines): zero tests — no mock executor, no HTTP handler test.

e2e/, regression/, viz/, indexer/ test directories are empty — planned infrastructure never populated.

HybridAlgorithmTest (81 lines) and BCQLParserTest (42 lines) provide some parser coverage.
QueryExecutor interface: all 6 methods declare 'throws IOException' — consistent error contract at the Java API level.

HTTP response JSON mixes camelCase and snake_case field names in the same objects: 'logDice' (camelCase) alongside 'seed_collocates_count', 'relation_type', 'avg_logdice', 'shared_by_count' (snake_case) — visible to all API consumers.

URL shape inconsistency: word sketch uses path parameters (/api/sketch/{lemma}/{relation}) while semantic field uses query parameters (/api/semantic-field/explore?seed=...&relation=...). The 'subject' of the query is encoded differently in sibling feature areas.

handleConcordanceExamples uses 'word1', 'word2' query params; semantic-field/examples uses 'adjective', 'noun'; semantic-field/explore uses 'seed'. Three different naming strategies for identifying the query subject across related endpoints.
All successful responses include 'status': 'ok' — consistent success envelope.
All error responses use the sendError() utility producing {'error': 'message'} JSON — consistent error shape.
handleBcqlQueryPost response uses 'hits' (count key) while handleConcordanceExamples uses 'count' — sibling concordance endpoints use different keys for the same concept.
The WordSketchApiServer public API is minimal and clean: only start() and stop() are public — good encapsulation.
Relation aliases are resolved via resolveRelationAlias() in ExplorationHandlers but the same alias map is not exposed or reused in SketchHandlers — siloed.
WordSketchApiServer binds to InetAddress.getLoopbackAddress() — all endpoints are restricted to localhost by network configuration; this is the sole access control mechanism.
No authentication middleware, session tokens, API keys, or permission checks exist anywhere in the codebase — consistently absent across all 10+ endpoints.

CORS header 'Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *' is set in every response (sendJsonResponse, sendError, sendBinaryResponse, sendOptionsResponse) — the wildcard CORS policy is consistently applied but contradicts the loopback security boundary for browser-based callers.
No sibling-endpoint inconsistency: there are no endpoints where some require auth and siblings do not — the policy is uniform (none).
No magic permission literals or role strings — no access control vocabulary exists at all.
The /health endpoint is a standard unauthenticated status check — intentional.
POST endpoints (/api/bcql, /api/visual/radial) handle OPTIONS preflight identically to how GET endpoints handle it via CORS headers — consistent method handling.
No audit logging, no user identity tracking, no admin-bypass patterns — the system has no user model at all.
No documentation or comment explicitly stating 'this service is intentionally unauthenticated and localhost-only' — the loopback binding is a code choice without prose rationale.

strengths: Three-tier layer stack (api / query / utils) has consistent, correctly-directed dependencies
QueryExecutor interface provides a stable separation between the HTTP layer and the BlackLab implementation
CqlUtils was properly extracted as a shared primitive to serve both PatternSubstitution and GrammarConfigLoader without duplication at the parsing level
Indexer subsystem is cleanly isolated under indexer/blacklab with no upward coupling into api
cross_module_architecture.issue_character: The dominant issue is that SemanticFieldExplorer conflates query orchestration with data model ownership, forcing the api layer to import inner result classes directly from the query package and making future model evolution a multi-file change.
cross_module_architecture.score_rationale: The codebase sits above the average anchor on architecture: the layer topology is sound, directions are correct, and the QueryExecutor interface provides a real seam. The score is limited to the low 60s because the inner-class DTO pattern in SemanticFieldExplorer creates a sustained boundary violation that is acknowledged but not resolved, and a utility method (escapeForRegex) is independently duplicated across two separate packages without a shared home.

strengths: Packages are named after domain concerns rather than technical roles — a new engineer can predict where corpus indexing vs. query logic lives
WordSketchApiServer enforces a single public entry point; handler classes are package-private, keeping internal API boundaries tight
QueryExecutor interface provides a clean seam that makes the api layer independent of BlackLab implementation details
Documentation (README, CLAUDE.md) accurately reflects the runtime structure
high_level_elegance.issue_character: SemanticFieldExplorer has grown into a multi-responsibility class that hosts both query orchestration logic and the data model for all semantic exploration results, making it a focal point of boundary confusion rather than a clean subsystem.
high_level_elegance.score_rationale: The codebase shows above-average structural intent: clearly named packages, single entry point, and a documented architecture that matches the code. The score is constrained to the low 60s because the result model is misplaced inside a query-layer class (forcing cross-package coupling), the utils package lacks cohesion, and SimpleTagger represents an orphaned abstraction with no active use path — together these blur ownership in the two most active subsystems.

strengths: GrammarConfigLoader.RelationConfig is a well-justified abstraction that encapsulates pattern manipulation, positional derivation, and POS group inference in one cohesive type
ExploreOptions record eliminates a 10-parameter method signature and gives the parameters a named, navigable shape
CqlUtils.splitCqlTokens is correctly extracted as a shared primitive serving two consuming packages without duplication
LogDiceCalculator provides a focused, mathematically clear formula with proper edge-case handling
abstraction_fitness.issue_character: The dominant abstraction problem is that HTTP response construction relies on raw Map<String,Object> bags across all handlers, while simultaneously some utility methods (alias methods in BlackLabSnippetParser, escapeForRegex copies) add indirection without adding value.
abstraction_fitness.score_rationale: The codebase's good abstractions (RelationConfig, ExploreOptions, CqlUtils) reflect intentional design, but the score is pulled down to the upper-50s by the systematic absence of typed response objects (every handler builds raw maps), the alias-method indirection in BlackLabSnippetParser that adds navigation cost without behavior, and the duplicated utility method across packages. Compared to an average codebase (~50), this is slightly above average in abstraction quality for domain logic but notably below on output/serialization discipline.

strengths: Production dependency surface is minimal (5 artifacts) with no redundancy
All library purposes are distinct: BlackLab (corpus search), fastjson2 (JSON), SLF4J (logging), JUnit (testing)
Version properties are centralized in pom.xml, preventing scatter
Test-scoped dependencies are correctly declared, keeping the runtime classpath clean
dependency_health.issue_character: The dependency set is lean and well-governed; the only concern is that slf4j-simple is an underpowered logging backend for a tool that is documented as a long-running REST server.
dependency_health.score_rationale: At a global anchor level this is a well-managed dependency set: no redundancy, no version conflicts visible, all artifacts are purposeful. The score is held below 85 because slf4j-simple is a deliberate but limiting choice for the server mode use case, and Maven's lack of lock-file pinning leaves transitive resolution unbounded — these are real but minor risks, not active defects.

strengths: LongIntHashMap implements a non-trivial data structure with correct invariants and no leaking internal state
convertConlluToWplChunks manages two interacting state variables with clean transitions and correct resource cleanup in finally
PatternSubstitution.substituteCollocate has direct, step-by-step control flow with each step serving a single purpose
LogDiceCalculator provides a mathematically clean formula implementation with precise zero-value guards
low_level_elegance.issue_character: The dominant low-level problem is that every HTTP handler method mixes parameter parsing, input validation, query dispatch, and manual map-based response construction in a single 70–120 line method body, making each handler method a monolith of mixed concerns at the function level.
low_level_elegance.score_rationale: The utility classes (LongIntHashMap, LogDiceCalculator, CqlUtils, PatternSubstitution) show good function-level discipline. However the handler layer — which is where most of the application's lines live — consistently violates the extraction principle by combining unrelated operations in long methods, and repeats the manual HashMap response-building pattern without any extraction. At a global anchor, the codebase sits slightly below average because the most-visited code paths (handlers) have the lowest internal discipline.

strengths: HttpApiUtils centralizes all HTTP response logic (sendJsonResponse, sendError, sendOptionsResponse, requireMethod) — callers never touch raw exchange mechanics.
QueryExecutor interface clearly documents the distinctions between its four query methods; the boundary between API and query layers is well-specified.
GrammarConfigLoader is received via constructor injection in all handlers, keeping the grammar-loading concern isolated in the config package.
CqlUtils.splitCqlTokens is properly extracted as a shared cross-layer primitive rather than duplicated inline.
resolveRelationAlias in ExplorationHandlers centralizes front-end alias translation in one method, avoiding scattered switch logic.
mid_level_elegance.issue_character: The dominant pattern is seam bleed: inner result classes from a query-layer class (SemanticFieldExplorer) are imported directly into the API layer, multi-seed orchestration logic is partially duplicated inside a handler rather than delegated to the explorer, and response serialization is embedded inline rather than at a dedicated translation boundary.
mid_level_elegance.score_rationale: The codebase clears a basic functionality bar — the QueryExecutor interface and HttpApiUtils provide two genuinely clean seams — but multiple handoff points are compromised. API handlers import query-layer inner classes (acknowledged TODO), one handler completely bypasses the semantic explorer for multi-seed logic, and response mapping is scattered across three large methods rather than a shared translation layer. These are recurring patterns at the API↔query boundary, not isolated incidents, placing the codebase in the lower half of the mid-tier range rather than approaching a well-factored score.

strengths: No restating comments — inline comments explain constraints and non-obvious decisions (fallback patterns, nullable contracts, algorithm steps) rather than echoing the code.
Defensive null/empty checks are concentrated at genuine API boundaries (HTTP handlers, executor entry points) and not applied to internal pure functions.
SemanticFieldExplorer and QueryExecutor Javadoc is substantive: explains algorithm strategy, positional hint semantics, and distinctions between related methods.
LogDiceCalculator compute(long,long,long) overload is a legitimate convenience shim, not a valueless pass-through.
ai_generated_debt.issue_character: The debt is narrow: a small number of thin private delegator methods and a repeated null-check + sendError pattern across handler methods suggest that common plumbing was written out verbatim rather than extracted — consistent with assisted authorship that didn't complete the consolidation step.
ai_generated_debt.score_rationale: The codebase avoids the most egregious AI-generated patterns: no entry/exit logging on every function, no try-catch around pure expressions, no generic name debt (handleData, processItem), and no docstring inflation on trivial methods. The identifiable debt is limited to a few valueless private delegator methods and repeated guard-clause boilerplate in handlers. This places the score in the mid-range — meaningfully better than a codebase with pervasive restating comments or defensive overengineering, but short of a score that would require the debt to be fully absent.

strengths: GrammarConfigLoader actively rejects the old 'copulas' top-level config key with a hard IOException, enforcing migration to the embedded-CQL pattern approach.
No @deprecated-but-active symbols, no 'remove after X' TODO comments, and no mixed-language files — the codebase has a clean single-stack foundation.

QueryExecutor interface is well-documented with explicit Javadoc distinguishing findCollocations from executeSurfacePattern and executeBcqlQuery, preventing accidental conflation of old vs new query pathways.
incomplete_migration.issue_character: The migration issues are all at the interface-vs-implementation seam: a convenience constructor retained alongside the preferred DI constructor, a method added to the concrete class but not the interface, and aliased method names in a parser utility where the old name was not retired when the canonical name was introduced.
incomplete_migration.score_rationale: This codebase shows no classic migration anti-patterns (deprecated symbols still called, feature-flag shims, old+new API frameworks coexisting). The observed issues are structural holdovers: a parallel convenience constructor explicitly discouraged in comments, a method not propagated to the interface, and a parser utility with both an old positional method and its canonical alias. These are real but mild; the codebase does not exhibit systematic abandonment of migration work. Score sits in the lower-good range — better than a codebase with active parallel API generations, but the dual-constructor risk (double-open) and dead extractCollocateFromMatchText utility lower confidence in completeness.

strengths: Package names (api, config, query, utils, indexer.blacklab) map clearly to contained responsibilities; no ambiguous folder naming.
No root-level straggler files — Main.java is the appropriate single root entry point.
utils package is internally cohesive: four low-level primitives (CqlUtils, LogDiceCalculator, LongIntHashMap, PosGroup) with no circular dependencies.
indexer.blacklab as a distinct sub-package isolates indexing from querying, which is the right boundary for two different corpus-lifecycle phases.
package_organization.issue_character: The primary issue is a missing model/ layer: seven result DTOs live as inner classes inside SemanticFieldExplorer (query package) and are imported by the api package, creating a coupling that the code itself acknowledges needs fixing. A secondary issue is PatternSubstitution's placement in api despite having no HTTP dependency and belonging functionally in utils.
package_organization.score_rationale: At 20 production files the project is small enough that most flat-package decisions are defensible. However, the SemanticFieldExplorer inner-class situation is a concrete structural deficiency: the api package imports query-layer inner types, creating a coupling direction that runs against the api→query layering. This is acknowledged in the code but unresolved. PatternSubstitution's misplacement in api is a secondary misfit. Single-file packages (tagging, viz) are low-value at this scale but not harmful. The score reflects a codebase with clear package intent but one unresolved boundary violation that affects navigability and coupling.

strengths: No class-level static initializers perform I/O or read environment state — all side-effectful initialization happens in constructors or factory methods called explicitly at startup.
GrammarConfigLoader.createDefaultEnglish() reads System.getProperty at call time in a static factory, not at class load time, avoiding fragile import-time environment coupling.
Main.handleServerCommand establishes a clear, ordered initialization sequence: executor → grammarConfig → server, each step explicit and exception-safe.
All classes with I/O in constructors (BlackLabQueryExecutor, GrammarConfigLoader) declare throws IOException, making failures visible rather than swallowed.
initialization_coupling.issue_character: The primary risk is not a classic import-time side-effect but a constructor-time heavy I/O operation (BlackLab index open) combined with a convenience constructor on SemanticFieldExplorer that can create a second index handle over the same path — a known risk acknowledged in comments but not enforced.
initialization_coupling.score_rationale: This codebase avoids the worst initialization-coupling patterns: no static-block I/O, no circular lazy imports, no env-var reads at class load time. The dominant concern is the SemanticFieldExplorer dual-constructor risk (double-open) and the general pattern of constructors doing heavy I/O, which reduces testability and makes boot failures opaque. These are real but manageable risks rather than systemic initialization coupling. The score reflects a codebase that handles initialization better than average but still carries a well-documented unresolved risk around index ownership.

strengths: Model package extraction is complete: ExplorationResult, DiscoveredNoun, CoreCollocate, AdjectiveProfile, Edge, ExploreOptions are cleanly separated in pl.marcinmilkowski.word_sketch.model
buildAndRankCollocates is a well-extracted private helper shared by multiple surface/dependency query paths
collectHits/scoreHits decomposition of executeBcqlQuery gives each phase a single clear responsibility
Handler-layer split into SketchHandlers and ExplorationHandlers appropriately partitions the API by concern
CqlUtils.splitCqlTokens exists as a shared primitive for CQL bracket-walking, even if it is not used everywhere it should be
design_coherence.issue_character: The dominant issue is mixed-abstraction-level methods: config objects carrying query-substitution logic, and large handler methods that inline JSON-response construction instead of delegating to formatters or the model layer.
design_coherence.score_rationale: The codebase scores in the low-50s range on a global anchor. The architectural skeleton (handler split, model package extraction) is sound, but GrammarConfigLoader.RelationConfig accumulates substitution responsibilities that belong in PatternSubstitution or CqlUtils, SemanticFieldExplorer.compare() fuses three distinct phases, and repeated inline response-map construction in handlers creates structural duplication. These are real maintainability liabilities rather than style issues, pulling the score below 60.

strengths: QueryExecutor interface Javadoc fully documents parameter semantics, return conditions, and IOException triggers for every method
ConcordanceResult.forSnippet factory communicates the 'plain snippet' contract cleanly: callers know exactly which fields will be null
getTotalFrequency Javadoc explicitly states 'Returns 0 if not found' and 'Logs and returns 0 on unexpected failures rather than propagating'
GrammarConfigLoader constructor validates all required fields (version, pattern, duplicate IDs) and throws IOException with descriptive messages on any contract violation
PosGroup.fromString returns OTHER rather than null or throwing on unknown input, which is an honest fallback contract
contract_coherence.issue_character: The predominant pattern is silent degradation: methods that encounter invalid inputs, unrecognized formats, or decode failures continue execution with defaults or fallbacks without surfacing the failure to callers, creating hidden contract violations.
contract_coherence.score_rationale: Java's static type system enforces most low-level contracts and several key types (PosGroup enum, RelationType enum, QueryExecutor interface) have explicit and accurate contracts. However, ConcordanceResult's nullable-everything constructor creates a partial-object anti-pattern, findCollocations' silent wildcard fallback breaks the method's implied contract, and toJson()'s NPE on a valid public-API code path are real contract holes. These bring the score below 70 on the global anchor.

strengths: LongIntHashMap is algorithmically correct with clear linear probing and no spurious conditions
LogDiceCalculator.compute has explicit guard clauses for all invalid-input cases before the log computation
Early-return guard clauses throughout handler methods (null/empty param checks returning 400) make the happy path unambiguous
CqlUtils.splitCqlTokens has simple and provably correct bracket-walking with clear quoted-string skipping
logic_clarity.issue_character: The issues are primarily dead or duplicated logic: unused computed variables, redundant null checks that cannot trigger, and duplicate implementations of the same algorithm in two places — none of these indicate bugs but they add cognitive load and maintenance surface.
logic_clarity.score_rationale: The core algorithmic logic (logDice, hash map, CQL parsing) is clear and correct. The issues are concentrated in the snippet-parsing and query-building layers: duplicate extraction methods with inconsistent lowercasing, a redundant null check that suggests mistaken copy-paste, and an unused computed variable in compare() that leaves a reader wondering if the logic is incomplete. These bring the score to low-60s on the global anchor — most paths are clear but several specific locations require readers to reason about redundant or dead code.

strengths: Java's static type system enforces all method signatures, generic types, and enum membership at compile time — the baseline type safety is high
PosGroup and RelationType enums are used for their natural purposes (dispatching, comparisons) rather than being bypassed with string literals in most places
ExploreOptions is a proper record with all non-null typed fields, eliminating parameter list ambiguity
QueryExecutor interface returns typed result lists, not raw Object or Map
LongIntHashMap, LogDiceCalculator are cleanly typed with primitive values avoiding boxing overhead and null uncertainty
type_safety.issue_character: The primary type-safety gap is at the API-response boundary: all HTTP responses are Map<String, Object>, making the JSON output shape completely invisible to the type checker; and within result DTOs, fields that are semantically absent are typed as nullable String or 0-value numerics rather than Optional or absent.
type_safety.score_rationale: Java provides strong compile-time type safety and the codebase uses it well for domain types (enums, records, generic collections). The score deductions come from the Map<String, Object> response pattern used across all ~15 handler methods (complete loss of type safety at the HTTP boundary), ConcordanceResult's nullable-everywhere DTOs, and the nullable posMap parameter. These are medium-severity issues: they don't cause compile errors but they do create runtime-only validation gaps. A score of 71 reflects 'mostly safe but with systematic gaps at API boundaries' on the global anchor.

strengths: Domain vocabulary (logDice, collocateLemma, deprel, xpos, bcql, headword) is accurate and used consistently across all layers, matching corpus linguistics conventions
Class names are precise and specific: BlackLabQueryExecutor, SemanticFieldExplorer, BlackLabSnippetParser, GrammarConfigLoader — each name is descriptive of its exact role
ExploreOptions record bundles what were previously spread parameters into a clearly named configuration object
Model package class names (DiscoveredNoun, CoreCollocate, AdjectiveProfile, ExplorationResult) clearly convey their roles in the exploration algorithm
naming_quality.issue_character: The naming issues cluster around method-level naming: inconsistent verb prefixes (find vs execute) for similar operations, multi-part compound method names where ordering buries the discriminating concept (SemanticFieldExploreMulti instead of MultiSeedExploration), and a generic method name (compare) that does not communicate its specific comparison subject.
naming_quality.score_rationale: Domain vocabulary and class/type names are genuinely good — a developer familiar with corpus linguistics can read the code's intent clearly. The deductions come from the find/execute inconsistency in BlackLabQueryExecutor (which creates ambiguity about which method to call), the append-suffix naming in ExplorationHandlers (handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti), and extractLemmaFromMatch/extractCollocateLemma naming both methods identically in behavior while implying different roles. These bring the score to mid-60s on the global anchor — notably above average but with real naming liabilities in the query and handler layers.

strengths: The query layer (BlackLabQueryExecutor) applies a single consistent strategy: wrap any library exception into IOException with original cause, propagate upward — no silent swallows at the execution level.
All API handlers validate required parameters and return 400 with descriptive messages before touching the executor.
HTTP-level error responses always use the same sendError(exchange, code, message) utility — format is consistent.
error_consistency.issue_character: The codebase has a well-designed error-propagation spine in the query layer, but the API routing layer has two failure modes: some routes swallow RuntimeExceptions silently, and one utility method (getTotalFrequency) returns a default value on failure instead of propagating, corrupting downstream logDice scores.
error_consistency.score_rationale: The query layer scores high on consistency, applying IOException wrapping uniformly. However, getTotalFrequency's silent-zero return is a meaningful correctness issue (not just a consistency smell), and the inconsistent RuntimeException coverage across sibling route wrappers creates unpredictable failure modes — some endpoints will surface errors as 500, others will silently fail. These two issues drag the score below 60. A fully consistent codebase would propagate or explicitly document every exception boundary.

strengths: Java naming conventions (PascalCase/camelCase) are applied uniformly across all ~20 production source files with zero drift.
Handler class structure is symmetric and consistent: both ExplorationHandlers and SketchHandlers follow identical patterns for construction, method naming, and HTTP utility usage.
Package decomposition is clean and navigable — a new developer can orient quickly.
convention_outlier.issue_character: The main convention outlier is a structural one: result DTOs are hosted as inner classes inside SemanticFieldExplorer rather than in the shared QueryResults class, creating an inconsistent pattern for result type placement that the codebase itself acknowledges as debt; a secondary issue is the HTTP JSON response field naming mixing camelCase and snake_case.
convention_outlier.score_rationale: The Java code-level conventions are very consistent, earning a high partial score. The score is pulled down by two real inconsistencies: the result DTO placement outlier (inner classes in SemanticFieldExplorer vs top-level in QueryResults) and the mixed camelCase/snake_case JSON response keys, which affect the external API contract. These are not trivial style issues — the DTO split creates import coupling and the JSON inconsistency is observable by all API consumers. This places the codebase above 'significant drift' (50) but well below 'fully consistent' (90).

strengths: Pure utility and parsing classes (BlackLabSnippetParser, LogDiceCalculator, PatternSubstitution, LongIntHashMap) are well-covered with focused unit tests.
SemanticFieldExplorer tests use a hand-crafted stub executor to isolate business logic from index access — demonstrates awareness of good test design.
All tests are assertion-based; no snapshot overuse or timing fragility observed.
test_strategy.issue_character: The test suite covers only the pure utility layer; every class that touches HTTP, routing, or index access — which together represent the majority of production-critical code — has zero test coverage.
test_strategy.score_rationale: The bottom third of the stack (parsers, calculators, data structures) is reasonably well tested. But the top two thirds — the HTTP handler layer, the query executor, CLI parsing, and grammar loading — are entirely untested. The empty e2e/ and regression/ directories indicate the gap was recognised but never addressed. Given that BlackLabQueryExecutor (545 lines, the only implementation of the QueryExecutor interface) has no unit tests and the handler classes have no mock-based tests, the coverage of critical paths is very low. This places the codebase below the midpoint for test_strategy.

strengths: QueryExecutor interface has consistent method signatures with uniform IOException contracts and clear Javadoc distinguishing each method's purpose.
All HTTP error responses use the same shape ({"error": message}) via a shared utility.
ExploreOptions record successfully prevents parameter-list explosion in the exploration pipeline.
api_surface_coherence.issue_character: The HTTP response JSON is the primary coherence problem: camelCase and snake_case field names coexist in the same response bodies across all endpoints, and sibling endpoints that perform concordance-style queries use different parameter names and count keys for the same concepts.
api_surface_coherence.score_rationale: The Java API layer (QueryExecutor interface, handler constructors) is reasonably coherent. The HTTP API surface — which is the outward contract for all clients — has two systematic inconsistencies: mixed JSON key casing and divergent parameter naming for the query subject across related endpoints. These are not isolated oversights; they affect multiple endpoints and response fields, making the API harder to consume programmatically. The score sits below average because the API shape issues are structural and client-visible, even though the Java internals are fairly clean.

strengths: The access control policy (localhost-only via loopback binding) is applied uniformly — no endpoint bypasses it.
CORS headers are consistently applied across all response paths through shared utilities, preventing accidental header omission.
No permission literals or role strings exist in any form — there is no partial or inconsistent permission model to audit.
authorization_consistency.issue_character: The codebase is a local research tool with a single, uniformly applied security posture (loopback binding, no auth), but the wildcard CORS header undermines the network boundary for browser clients, and the security design choice is undocumented.
authorization_consistency.score_rationale: From an authorization_consistency perspective, this codebase scores well because the policy is uniform — there are no sibling endpoints with mismatched auth, no magic permission strings, and no partial middleware. The score is not higher because the wildcard CORS header creates an inconsistency between the intended security boundary (loopback only) and what browsers are actually permitted to do (any origin can make cross-origin requests to the API if it resolves), and because the design intent is not documented anywhere. A fully consistent codebase at this level would either restrict CORS to localhost origins or document the tradeoff.
